banner: Week 3 · Planes & the Language of Sets
essential_question: What do you get when a line spreads sideways — and how do mathematicians talk about groups of things?
objective: Define a plane and identify coplanar points, and use set notation (∪, ∩, ⊂, ∅) to combine and compare sets.

content.prompt: A line has no width — just length. Push it sideways forever. What do you get? And can two flat surfaces ever touch at just one single point?

agenda.title: I do — Planes, Coplanar, and the Set Symbols

content.heading: Two dimensions, and four new symbols
bullets: A plane is flat, has length AND width (two-dimensional), and extends forever in every direction.
Name a plane one of two ways: a single letter (plane M) or three points not all on one line (plane ABC).
Points or lines that lie in the same plane are coplanar.
A set is a collection of things; the empty set (∅) contains nothing.
Union (∪) combines everything in two sets; intersection (∩) is only the overlap; subset (⊂) means fully contained.

content.heading: Toggle the sets; watch the highlight change
content.note: On the sim, click Union, Intersection, A only, and B only in turn. Say out loud, in your own words, what region lights up each time and why.

agenda.title: You do — Sets on the Worksheet

content.preview: Build session: hunt for a real plane and sort real objects into overlapping sets.

success_criteria.text: I can define a plane and name it two ways, and identify coplanar points
I can find the union and intersection of two sets and use ∪, ∩, ⊂, ∅ correctly
I can state what two lines intersect in and what two planes intersect in

questions.explain: A plane has length AND width — two dimensions — but no thickness.

questions.prompt: The intersection of two distinct lines that cross is always a…
questions.explain: Two straight, non-parallel lines can only cross at one shared location.

questions.prompt: Give two correct ways to name a plane.
questions.answer: A single capital letter (e.g., plane M), or three points in it that are not all on one line (e.g., plane ABC).
questions.explain: Either a single letter or three non-collinear points uniquely identifies one flat plane.

questions.prompt: Points and lines that lie in the same plane are called…
questions.explain: Coplanar describes sharing one plane, the same way collinear describes sharing one line.

questions.prompt: Let A = {2, 4, 6, 8} and B = {4, 8, 12}. Find A ∪ B and A ∩ B.
questions.explain: Union combines every element from both sets, writing shared members once; intersection keeps only the members that appear in both lists (4 and 8).

questions.prompt: Draw two overlapping circles labeled Set A and Set B. Shade only the region that represents A ∩ B.
questions.answer: Two overlapping circles labeled A and B, with only the lens-shaped overlap shaded.
questions.explain: Rubric: two clearly overlapping circles labeled A and B (1 pt) + only the shared overlap region shaded, not the crescents (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Exit: name one real object made of two overlapping groups (e.g., 'toys that are round' and 'toys that are blue') and say what belongs in the intersection.
